title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. With FAVs (level 5 automation), interpersonal communication between pedestrians and drivers is not possible, necessitating designed interfaces for positive pedestrian-FAV interaction.
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using HTC Vive headset with Unity 5 simulated environment. 30 participants completed 16 trials (4 visual x 4 audible features in randomized complete block design) walking across a virtual crosswalk as a FAV approached and stopped. Features tested: visual (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, no signal) and audible (horn, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal). Collected both objective measures (crossing time, waiting time via headset tracking) and subjective measures (receptivity surveys, feature ratings, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment / Human-subjects laboratory experiment
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision at crosswalk with approaching FAV; Feature preference evaluation; Receptivity assessment before/after exposure
datasets: Custom VR pedestrian simulator developed in Unity 5; Pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ) - 20-item short version; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Personal Innovativeness Scale
metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 scale); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before crossing; Pedestrian receptivity scores; Personal innovativeness scores; PBQ subscales: violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors
claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with inclusion of external features
notes: VR platform validated in prior study (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017) confirming participants' walking speeds matched published literature; Participants had normal/corrected vision, no hearing problems, no simulation sickness; Only 2 participants had prior VR experience; Walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/sec) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/sec), possibly due to uncertainty with driverless vehicle; Verbal message and music had significant effects on crossing time; beep confused participants more than no signal; Pedestrians who violate rules intentionally and those who get distracted became more cautious with FAVs - potential traffic safety enhancement

notes: VR platform validated in prior study (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017) confirming participants' walking speeds matched published literature; Participants had normal/corrected vision, no hearing problems, no simulation sickness; Only 2 participants had prior VR experience; Walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/sec) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/sec), possibly due to uncertainty with driverless vehicle; Verbal message and music had significant effects on crossing time; beep confused participants more than no signal; Pedestrians who violate rules intentionally and those who get distracted became more cautious with FAVs - potential traffic safety enhancement

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: This study investigates the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The research addresses the need to understand why pedestrians run red-lights to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related crashes.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey was conducted in Hefei, China collecting pedestrians' socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
tasks: Predict probability of pedestrian red-light running behavior; Identify significant factors affecting red-light running
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections)
metrics: Score test p-values; Odds ratios; Model coefficients; 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), and results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | The study only considered pedestrian red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types | The study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Limited sample size and time-labor constraints may affect generalizability to other contexts
notes: The study uses a binomial logistic model with Score test for variable selection; Gender and attitude about waiting with elders/children were not significant factors; Key significant factors include: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period (daytime highest risk), attitude about running red in hurry, and attitude about road facility quality; The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the original paper; Data collection occurred during peak periods (7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm) on weekdays and weekends during May-June 2015

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: This study investigates the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections)
metrics: Score test p-values; Odds ratios; Model coefficients; 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Strolling pedestrians have lower red-light running probability (odds ratio 0.394) compared to those going to work or school, indicating more safe crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), and results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | The study only considered pedestrian red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types | The study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Limited sample size and time-labor constraints may affect generalizability to other contexts
notes: The study uses a binomial logistic model with Score test for variable selection; Gender and attitude about waiting with elders/children were not significant factors; Key significant factors include: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period (daytime highest risk), attitude about running red in hurry, and attitude about road facility quality; The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the original paper; Data collection occurred during peak periods (7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm) on weekdays and weekends during May-June 2015

authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: This study investigates the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections)
metrics: Score test p-values; Odds ratios; Model coefficients; 95% confidence intervals

limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), and results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | The study only considered pedestrian red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types | The study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Limited sample size and time-labor constraints may affect generalizability to other contexts
notes: The study uses a binomial logistic model with Score test for variable selection; Gender and attitude about waiting with elders/children were not significant factors; Key significant factors include: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period (daytime highest risk), attitude about running red in hurry, and attitude about road facility quality; The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the original paper; Data collection occurred during peak periods (7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm) on weekdays and weekends during May-June 2015

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: This study investigates the factors that influence pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The research addresses the need to understand why pedestrians run red-lights to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related crashes.

Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
tasks: Predict probability of pedestrian red-light running behavior; Identify significant factors affecting red-light running
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections)
metrics: Score test p-values; Odds ratios; Model coefficients; 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians who are affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red lights at intersections (odds ratio 0.483 for unaffected group)
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), and results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | The study only considered pedestrian red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types | The study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons or group effects | Limited sample size and time-labor constraints may affect generalizability to other contexts
notes: The study uses a binomial logistic model with Score test for variable selection; Gender and attitude about waiting with elders/children were not significant factors; Key significant factors include: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period (daytime highest risk), attitude about running red in hurry, and attitude about road facility quality; The probability prediction function is provided in Equation 3 of the original paper; Data collection occurred during peak periods (7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm) on weekdays and weekends during May-June 2015

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to capture crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity and interaction effects
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running decision-making; measure effects of personal factors (gender, age, companion presence), environmental factors (signal timing, traffic volume), and social influences (behavior of other pedestrians)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed across 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hotspots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.667), with the random component of gender effect being statistically significant
limitations: The study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (e.g., education level) are not available in the observation survey

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to capture crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity and interaction effects
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running decision-making; measure effects of personal factors (gender, age, companion presence), environmental factors (signal timing, traffic volume), and social influences (behavior of other pedestrians)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed across 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hotspots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison
claim_text: Presence of a companion (b=-0.984) and increase in number of pedestrians waiting (b=-0.394) both significantly reduce red light running propensity, reflecting influence of social norms
limitations: The study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (e.g., education level) are not available in the observation survey

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to capture crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity and interaction effects
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running decision-making; measure effects of personal factors (gender, age, companion presence), environmental factors (signal timing, traffic volume), and social influences (behavior of other pedestrians)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed across 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hotspots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison
claim_text: When other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle, individual red light running propensity increases significantly (b=1.029), indicating vicarious experience of punishment avoidance
limitations: The study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (e.g., education level) are not available in the observation survey

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to capture crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity and interaction effects
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running decision-making; measure effects of personal factors (gender, age, companion presence), environmental factors (signal timing, traffic volume), and social influences (behavior of other pedestrians)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed across 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hotspots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison
claim_text: Older pedestrians have higher red light running propensity (b=0.174) compared to adults, contrary to some previous studies, possibly due to lower educational attainment and cognitive abilities in Hong Kong
limitations: The study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (e.g., education level) are not available in the observation survey

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)

A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity and interaction effects

datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed across 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong (hotspots with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison
claim_text: Significant interaction effects exist: gender moderates the effect of traffic volume and others' violations; elderly behavior is moderated by companion presence and others' violations
limitations: The study focuses on red light running behavior but does not assess safety outcomes (i.e., associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not measured, though it is correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (e.g., education level) are not available in the observation survey
